event: Early settlers, likely from southern India and Sri Lanka, begin inhabiting the Maldivian atolls.
Indo-Aryan and Dravidian seafarers establish more permanent coastal communities, laying the basis for Maldivian culture and language.
Buddhism spreads to the islands following missionary activity associated with the Mauryan emperor Ashoka, becoming the dominant religion.
Greek geographer Ptolemy references islands believed to be the Maldives, placing them on early Indian Ocean trade routes.
An embassy from the islands is recorded reaching the Roman emperor Julian, reflecting long-distance maritime contact.
Theravada Buddhism flourishes, with stupas and monasteries (hawittas) built across the atolls during the Buddhist era.
Chinese Tang dynasty records note tribute from the islands, attesting to early diplomatic and trade links with China.
Arab traveler al-Masudi describes the islands' cowrie-shell economy and woman-ruled polities.
Royal Lomafanu copperplate grants begin to be issued, documenting land donations during the late Buddhist kingdom.
King Dhovemi converts to Islam, traditionally credited to the missionary Abu al-Barakat, founding the Maldives as a Muslim sultanate.
The Theemuge dynasty consolidates the Islamic sultanate and Arabic-influenced administration takes root.
The visiting traveler Ibn Battuta serves as a qadi (judge) in the Maldives, leaving a detailed account of its society and rulers.
Queen Khadijah reigns over the sultanate, one of several women to lead the islands during the medieval period.
Ming dynasty admiral Zheng He's fleet visits the Maldives during its Indian Ocean voyages, strengthening ties with China.
Portuguese forces from Goa begin attempts to establish control over the strategically located islands.
The Portuguese capture Male and impose colonial rule over the Maldives.
Muhammad Thakurufaanu leads a rebellion that expels the Portuguese, an event commemorated as National Day.
The Maldives establishes a tributary relationship with the Dutch rulers of Ceylon in exchange for protection.
Raiders from the Ali Rajas of Cannanore (Malabar) briefly seize Male before being driven out.
Sultan Hassan Nooraddeen I founds the Huraa dynasty, which would rule until the end of the monarchy.
Britain takes control of Ceylon, gradually bringing the Maldives within its sphere of influence.
The Maldives becomes a British protectorate, retaining internal self-rule while ceding defence and foreign affairs.
The Maldives adopts its first written constitution, formalizing the sultanate's governance.
The sultanate is briefly abolished and a first republic declared under President Mohamed Amin Didi, before the monarchy is restored months later.
The southern atolls secede to form the breakaway United Suvadive Republic, which lasts until 1963.
The Maldives gains full independence from Britain on 26 July and joins the United Nations on 21 September.
A national referendum abolishes the sultanate, and the Second Republic is proclaimed with Ibrahim Nasir as president.
The first tourist resort opens, launching the tourism industry that would transform the Maldivian economy.
Maumoon Abdul Gayoom becomes president, beginning a 30-year rule that was among the longest in Asia.
The Maldives joins the Commonwealth of Nations.
Indian military intervention (Operation Cactus) defeats a coup attempt by Maldivian dissidents and Sri Lankan Tamil mercenaries.
The Maldives hosts the Small States Conference on Sea Level Rise, becoming an early global voice on climate change.
A revised constitution strengthens the presidency as Gayoom continues to dominate the political system.
The Indian Ocean tsunami devastates many islands, killing scores and causing widespread destruction across the atolls.
Parliament votes to allow a multiparty political system, ending decades of one-party dominance.
A new democratic constitution is adopted, and Mohamed Nasheed wins the first multiparty presidential election.
President Nasheed holds a televised underwater cabinet meeting to highlight the threat of rising sea levels.
President Nasheed resigns amid a police and military mutiny he later describes as a coup, and Mohamed Waheed becomes president.
Abdulla Yameen wins a contested presidential election after a turbulent and repeatedly delayed vote.
Former president Nasheed is convicted under anti-terrorism laws in a trial widely condemned as politically motivated.
The Maldives announces its withdrawal from the Commonwealth amid criticism of its human-rights record.
Ibrahim Mohamed Solih wins the presidential election, defeating Yameen in a major opposition victory.
The Maldives rejoins the Commonwealth of Nations.
Mohamed Muizzu wins the presidency on a platform emphasizing closer ties with China and an 'India Out' stance.
President Muizzu's party wins a parliamentary supermajority and Indian military personnel are withdrawn from the islands.
